name: Cleveland-Cliffs Inc.

profile.description: Cleveland-Cliffs Inc. is the largest flat-rolled steel producer in North America, with approximately 30 million net tons of annual steelmaking capacity. Through four major acquisitions (AK Steel, ArcelorMittal USA, Ferrous Processing & Trading, and Stelco), the company has transformed from an iron ore miner into a vertically integrated steel producer serving automotive, infrastructure, and manufacturing markets. The company also mines iron ore and produces pellets, HBI, and coke. Founded in 1847, headquartered in Cleveland, Ohio.

innovation.label: DEVELOPING
innovation.analysis: Track Record (2/3): CEO Lourenco Goncalves executed one of the most dramatic transformations in industrial history — converting a niche iron ore miner into North America's largest flat-rolled steel producer through four major acquisitions in four years (AK Steel 2020, ArcelorMittal USA 2020, FPT 2021, Stelco 2024) [2]. The company is the sole Great Lakes HBI producer, enabling lower-emission steelmaking. However, the cancelled $500M green steel project in 2025 tempers the innovation narrative. AI Deployment (0/2): No meaningful AI deployment with measurable results. The company uses basic process optimization and predictive maintenance tools common across heavy industry, but no AI-powered products or quantified AI savings have been disclosed. Market Share (2/2): Cleveland-Cliffs is the dominant flat-rolled steel producer in North America with ~30M net tons of capacity. The company holds the largest position in automotive steel supply, with fixed-price contracts signed with all major OEMs [1]. Product Pipeline (0/2): No next-generation products with specific timelines announced. The POSCO partnership for EV-grade advanced high-strength steel remains in negotiation without confirmed dates. R&D Intensity (1/1): The company's investment in HBI production, iron ore pelletization, and steelmaking process improvements represents above-average capital allocation to production technology for a steel producer.

inspiration.label: DEVELOPING
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (3/4): Goncalves has articulated a specific and thoughtful purpose — rebuilding American steel manufacturing, securing the domestic supply chain, and protecting American manufacturing jobs [2]. This mission clearly shapes strategic decisions: aggressive domestic acquisitions, vocal tariff advocacy, union partnership (rather than antagonism), and rejection of foreign ownership. The vision is validated by a decade of consistent execution. Goncalves stated: 'You don't need to be a union company to be a good company, but it's very easy to be a bad company when you don't have a union' — reflecting genuine conviction [2]. Community Impact (1/2): Cleveland-Cliffs is often the largest employer in its operating communities (Cleveland, Pittsburgh, and steel towns across the Midwest). The company donated $7.5M in charitable contributions in 2023, and the Stelco acquisition preserved Canadian steelmaking jobs [3]. However, specific programs are modest relative to the company's scale. Employee Retention (1/2): Goncalves has maintained a stable core leadership team through four major acquisitions, and the company has integrated 25,000+ employees across diverse facilities without catastrophic cultural failures. The union-friendly approach fosters workforce loyalty, with successfully negotiated labor contracts covering employees across numerous operations [3]. Insider Buying (1/2): An insider purchased 19,700 shares ($200K) on February 13, 2026 at $10.13. However, a major insider sale of 3,000,000 shares ($37.3M) occurred on February 11, 2026, and another 200,000 shares ($2.1M) sold on February 17, making net insider activity heavily negative.

infrastructure.label: DEVELOPING
infrastructure.analysis: Mechanically scored on available metrics. Cleveland-Cliffs reported Q1 2026 revenue of $4.9B (up $600M QoQ) with a narrowed net loss of $237M — improving but still unprofitable [1]. The recovery is driven by HRC steel prices averaging $980/ton (up 24% YoY), supported by tariff-driven import reductions and improving domestic demand. Full-year 2026 guidance targets 16.5-17.0M net tons of shipments with $10/ton unit cost reductions. D/E of 129% reflects $7.8B debt against $45M cash — a severely levered balance sheet. ROE is deeply negative at -18.6% and profit margin is -6.4%, both penalized by two consecutive years of net losses ($760M in 2024, $1.48B in 2025). Forward P/E of 21.9 is modestly above the 20 target. PEG of 0.43 is attractive, reflecting expected earnings recovery. Revenue growth YoY of 6.3% shows early inflection, but the 5yr revenue CAGR of -6.9% falls below the floor.

ai_resilience.label: LOW RISK
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (8/9): Steel manufacturing is one of the most AI-immune industries in existence. Converting iron ore into flat-rolled steel through blast furnaces, basic oxygen furnaces, or electric arc furnaces is a capital-intensive, physical-world process that AI cannot substitute [1][2]. Cleveland-Cliffs' vertically integrated operations — from iron ore mining through pelletization, HBI production, primary steelmaking, and downstream finishing — involve massive physical infrastructure, complex metallurgy, and union labor that have no AI replacement. AI adoption in data centers, automotive, infrastructure, and manufacturing actually increases steel demand (server racks, construction materials, EV bodies). Tariff policy, not AI, is the primary risk factor cited by analysts [1]. AI Opportunity (0/1): No AI-generated revenue. The company uses standard industrial process optimization tools but does not sell AI products or services.

infinite_game.summary: Goncalves has demonstrated extraordinary existential flexibility in transforming Cleveland-Cliffs from an iron ore miner into North America's steel champion, backed by genuine conviction about American manufacturing's importance. His union-friendly approach and decade of consistent strategic execution reflect authentic purpose. However, his combative posture toward US Steel, political tariff advocacy, and aggressive insider selling create tension with infinite game principles. The company plays to endure, but Goncalves often plays to win.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (2/3): Goncalves's cause — rebuilding American steel manufacturing and securing domestic supply chains — is genuine, service-oriented, and validated by a decade of consistent execution through four transformative acquisitions [2]. His explicit support for organized labor ('it's very easy to be a bad company without a union') and advocacy for domestic steel policy reflects conviction beyond shareholder value maximization. Strategic decisions clearly trace to this purpose. However, the cause is more national-industrial than visionary, and the political framing ('American steel first') introduces finite game elements. It hasn't been tested through a leadership transition.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (1/2): Goncalves has maintained a core leadership team through four major acquisitions and successfully integrated 25,000+ employees across diverse facilities [2]. His union-friendly approach — proactively negotiating new labor contracts covering employees at numerous operations — creates structural trust with the workforce [3]. S&P Global Platts recognized him as 'admired and followed by employees.' However, his combative public persona and the heavy industry culture limits full psychological safety at scale.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (0/1): Goncalves's competitive language is consistently combative — his public attacks on US Steel, foreign steel producers, and import competition reflect a finite 'win or lose' mindset. His tariff advocacy frames competition as a war to be won through political protection rather than self-improvement [2]. While the domestic manufacturing conviction is genuine, the competitive approach lacks the humility and learning orientation that Sinek's Worthy Rivals framework requires.

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (2/2): The transformation from a struggling iron ore miner into North America's largest flat-rolled steel producer through four acquisitions in four years is a textbook Sinek example of existential flexibility [2]. Each acquisition — AK Steel, ArcelorMittal USA, FPT, Stelco — fundamentally changed the company's identity and capabilities. The pivot was driven by Goncalves's vision of domestic steel leadership, not desperation, and required betting the company multiple times.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (1/2): Acquiring ArcelorMittal USA for $1.4B during the COVID pandemic, maintaining capex through the 2024-2025 steel downcycle while reporting $2.2B in cumulative net losses, and continuing to invest in HBI and facility improvements demonstrate genuine courage [1][2]. However, the cancelled $500M green steel project signals limits to courageous investment when financial pressure mounts, and massive insider selling ($37M+ in February 2026) undermines the narrative of leadership conviction.

investment_thesis: Cleveland-Cliffs is a cyclical turnaround with the wind at its back. Q1 2026 revenue of $4.9B (up $600M QoQ) was driven by HRC prices averaging $980/ton (24% YoY increase), with the CEO declaring the 'order book is full' [1]. U.S. steel tariffs have materially reduced imports, driving domestic demand and pricing power. The slab contract termination delivers an estimated $500M annual EBITDA benefit. Full-year shipment guidance of 16.5-17.0M net tons with $10/ton cost reductions points to meaningful profitability recovery. At $10.15 per share with a PEG of 0.43 and $5.8B market cap against $18.9B in revenue, the stock is pricing in no recovery — any return to normalized margins would drive substantial re-rating.
thesis_continued: The bear case is that two consecutive years of net losses ($760M in 2024, $1.48B in 2025) have severely damaged the balance sheet — D/E of 129% with $7.8B debt against just $45M cash creates acute financial vulnerability if the recovery stalls [1]. The tariff-dependent business model means any policy shift could collapse domestic steel pricing overnight. Automotive exposure (~30% of revenue) faces EV transition uncertainty that may suppress steel demand for traditional body-in-white applications. CEO Goncalves's $37M+ insider selling in February 2026 contradicts the bullish narrative, and the POSCO partnership remains unconfirmed. The 5yr revenue CAGR of -6.9% reflects a structurally declining top line despite acquisitions, and free cash flow is negative at -$60.5M TTM. At $10/share, the market is not wrong to demand proof of recovery before re-rating [3].
key_catalysts: HRC steel prices sustaining above $1,000/ton through H2 2026 as tariff enforcement and reshoring activity drive domestic demand, delivering return to GAAP profitability
Slab contract termination generating $500M+ annual EBITDA benefit, transforming the cost structure and enabling deleveraging from the current 129% D/E ratio
Definitive POSCO partnership agreement providing access to EV-grade advanced high-strength steel and diversifying revenue beyond traditional automotive applications
key_risks: Balance sheet fragility: $7.8B debt against $45M cash and negative FCF creates existential risk if steel prices decline or automotive demand fails to recover in 2026
Tariff policy dependence: any relaxation, exemption, or trade deal that reopens U.S. markets to imported steel could collapse domestic HRC pricing from $980/ton toward $700-800/ton
CEO Goncalves's $37M+ insider selling in February 2026, combined with combative public persona and aggressive acquisition strategy, creates corporate governance risk and potential succession concerns
